Back: A network of nerve cells that transmits electrical signals throughout the body.

Front: What are the two main parts of the nervous system?
Back: Central nervous system and peripheral nervous system.

Front: What is the central nervous system?
Back: The brain and spinal cord.

Front: What is the peripheral nervous system?
Back: All nerves outside the brain and spinal cord.
A specialised cell that transmits nerve impulses.

Front: What are the three types of neurones?
Back: Sensory neurones, relay neurones and motor neurones.
Carries impulses from receptors to the CNS.
Carries impulses from the CNS to effectors.
Connects sensory and motor neurones in the CNS.
A cell that detects stimuli.
A detectable change in the environment.
A reaction to a stimulus.
Muscles or glands that produce responses.
A rapid automatic response to a stimulus.
The pathway taken by nerve impulses during a reflex.

Front: What are the steps in a reflex arc?
Back: Stimulus, receptor, sensory neurone, relay neurone, motor neurone, effector.
Part of a neurone that receives impulses.
Long fibre carrying nerve impulses away from the cell body.
Part of neurone containing nucleus and organelles.
Fatty layer insulating the axon.

Front: What is the function of myelin sheath?
Back: Speeds up transmission of nerve impulses.
Gaps in the myelin sheath.
Impulse jumping between nodes of Ranvier.
Electrical charge across the membrane when neurone is inactive.

Front: What is the typical resting potential value?
Back: About -70 mV.
Unequal distribution of ions across membrane.

Front: What ions are involved in nerve impulses?
Back: Sodium and potassium ions.
Rapid change in membrane potential during impulse transmission.
Sodium channels open and Na+ enters the neurone.
Potassium ions leave the neurone.
Short period where neurone cannot fire again.

Front: What is the importance of the refractory period?
Back: Ensures impulses travel in one direction.
The junction between two neurones.
Gap between neurones.
Chemical messengers transmitting signals across synapses.

Front: What neurotransmitter is commonly studied in A-level biology?
Back: Acetylcholine.

Front: What happens when an impulse reaches a synapse?
Back: Vesicles release neurotransmitters.
Exocytosis.

Front: What happens to neurotransmitters after release?
Back: Diffuse across synaptic cleft.

Front: What happens when neurotransmitter reaches postsynaptic membrane?
Back: Binds to receptors.

Front: What happens when receptors are activated?
Back: Ion channels open.
Triggers a new action potential.

Front: What happens to neurotransmitters after signalling?
Back: Broken down by enzymes.
Acetylcholinesterase.
Combining multiple signals to trigger an action potential.
Multiple neurones firing simultaneously.
Rapid firing of one neurone.
Myelin sheath and axon diameter.
Myelination.

Front: What is the role of sodium-potassium pump?
Back: Maintains ion gradients.
Minimum potential needed to trigger an action potential.
Action potentials either occur fully or not at all.
Conversion of stimulus energy into nerve impulses.

Front: What is the role of the brain?
Back: Processing and coordinating responses.
Transmits impulses between brain and body.
Pressure receptor in the skin.

Front: What stimulus does pacinian corpuscle detect?
Back: Mechanical pressure.

Front: What happens when pacinian corpuscle is stimulated?
Back: Membrane deforms triggering nerve impulse.
Initial electrical signal produced by receptor.

Front: What happens if generator potential reaches threshold?
Back: Action potential forms.
Movement of action potentials along neurones.

Front: What ensures fast communication in the body?
Back: Electrical impulses in neurones.
